content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 5, 1991


G. Victor Leipzig, Ph.D.
Executive Director
The Bolsa Chica Conservancy
P. O. Box 3976
Huntington Beach, CA 92605

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-120

Dear Mr. Leipzig:

This letter is in response to your request for advice
concerning your duties and responsibilities under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Your request does not address one particular decision before your
agency but rather seeks general guidance. Accordingly, we treat
your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to the
provisions of Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed).²

QUESTIONS

1. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
disqualify you from participating in decisions when the Bolsa
Chica Foundation is directly before the planning commission?

2. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
disqualify you from participating in decisions affecting the Koll
Company which has made significant financial contributions to the
Bolsa Chica Conservancy?

3. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
disqualify you from participating in decisions affecting firms or

__________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-120
Page No. 2

individuals who have made smaller financial donations to the Bolsa
Chica Conservancy?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act disqualify
you from participating in decisions which will directly or
indirectly affect the Bolsa Chica Conservancy.

2 and 3. You will not be disqualified from participating in
decisions affecting individuals or entities which have made
donations to the Bolsa Chica Conservancy unless the decision will
have a material financial effect on the conservancy.

FACTS

You are a planning commissioner for the City of Huntington
Beach. You also serve as the executive director of the Bolsa
Chica Conservancy (the "conservancy"). The conservancy is a
nonprofit corporation which conducts environmental research and
education programs. You receive compensation from the conservancy
for the discharge of your duties as a part-time employee. The
conservancy does not own land and it does not engage in commercial
activities.

Local firms and individuals have made donations to the
conservancy. You have stated for advice that such
contributions indirectly affect you financially. Your salary is
not paid to you directly by the Koll Company. However, the Koll
Company has made significant financial contributions to the
conservancy. The Koll Company is the primary land owner in the
Bolsa Chica area and engages in land development. An employee of
this company sits on the board of directors of the conservancy.
You expect that the Koll Company will appear before the planning
commission during your term of office. Other firms and
individuals also make smaller financial contributions to the
conservancy.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making, or in any way using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest. A planning commissioner is a public official.
(Section 82048.)

A public official has a financial interest in a decision if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a

Our File No. I-91-120
Page No. 3

material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on the official, on a member of the official's
immediate family³ or on:

Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

Section 87103(c).

Your facts indicate that the conservancy is a source of
income to you. For purposes of our analysis we assume that this
income exceeds $250 in a 12-month period. Accordingly, you must
disqualify yourself from participating in decisions which will
foreseeably and materially affect the conservancy.

Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed) provides that the effect
of a decision is material if a source of income to a public
official of $250 or more in the preceding 12 months is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency or there is a
nexus between the purpose for which the official receives income
and the governmental decision. A source of income to a public
official is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency or entity, either personally or by an
agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's agency.

(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.

Regulation 18702.1(b).


³ An official's "immediate family" consists of his or her spouse
and dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Our File No. I-91-120
Page No. 4

As you correctly state in your request for advice, if the
conservancy appeared before the planning commission, you would
have to disqualify yourself from participating in such decisions.
You would also be disqualified from participating in decisions
where there is a nexus between your position as executive director
of the conservancy and your official duties in the planning
commission. This would be the case, for example, if the planning
commission endorsed a particular item of environmental research
prepared by the conservancy. This would also be the case if the
conservancy were to take a position on a matter pending before the
planning commission. (Freers Advice Letter, No. A-86-281, copy
enclosed.)

Your disqualification would also be required if the
conservancy were affected indirectly by the decision. This would
be the case, for example, if a decision pending before the
planning commission would affect a real property interest held by
the conservancy, such as a lease.

When a nonprofit entity is indirectly affected by a decision,
the standard for determining materiality is that of Regulation
18702.5 (copy enclosed). For example, if the nonprofit has gross
annual receipts of $100,000 or less, the effect of a decision will
be material if

(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease of the entity's gross annual receipts
for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 or more.

(2) The decision will cause the entity to
incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or
eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in
the amount of $2,500 or more.

(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the entity's assets or
liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more.

Regulation 18702.5(f).

Therefore, if you determine that a decision before the
planning commission will indirectly affect the conservancy, your
disqualification is required if the decision will affect the
conservancy in the amounts indicated above.

You have also asked whether your disqualification is required
when an individual or a business entity which has made a gift to
the conservancy appears before the planning commission. As a

Our File No. I-91-120
Page No. 5

general rule, donors to the nonprofit conservancy would not be
sources of income to you. (Dean Advice Letter, No. A-89-559, copy
enclosed.) However, you disqualification would be required if it
is foreseeable that a decision affecting a source of gifts to the
conservancy would result in an increase or decrease in the income
of the conservancy by the amounts set forth in the applicable
provisions of Regulation 18702.5, discussed above. For example,
you have indicated that the Koll Company is a major contributor to
the conservancy. If it is foreseeable that a decision affecting
Koll Company might result in a material reduction in the funding
of the conservancy, then your disqualification would be required.
Otherwise, as stated above, individuals or entities which make
donations to the conservancy are not disqualifying sources of
income to you.

     We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Blanca M. Breeze
                                             Counsel, Legal Division
